coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of aldehyde-functionalized dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of bisulfite in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Cui Cui
Xu, Zi Yi
Sun, Zhe
Xie, Jia Hao
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: Bisulfite (HSO3 −) is used as a food additive for its antibacterial and antioxidant properties. However, excessive intake of HSO3 − is harmful to humans. Here, for the first time, aldehyde-functionalized dual-emissive carbon dots (D-CDs) are synthesized in one-step for direct ratiometric sensing of HSO3 −. Due to the nucleophilic addition reaction between HSO3 − and aldehyde of D-CDs, the fluorescence transforms from green to deep-blue. The linear range of the probe is 0.1–30 μmol/L with a detection limit of 42 nmol/L. Moreover, D-CDs show good selectivity and a fast reaction time (<5 min) toward HSO3 −. The probe has been applied to trace HSO3 − detection in food samples. The recoveries range from 96.5 % to 107.0 % with relative standard deviations below 6.5 %. In addition, a smartphone sensing platform has been designed, which provides a wider application prospect for the real-time monitoring of HSO3 − in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aldehyde functional D-CDs
Bisulfite
Ratiometric sensing
Food samples
Smartphone imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification procedures for aldehyde-functionalized D-CDs
Procedures for ratiometric fluorescence determination of HSO3 −
Measurement of HSO3 − in food samples
Study on the optical properties of D-CDs
Spectral responses of D-CDs toward HSO3 −

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj- zdxmX0003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and diameter distribution (inset) of D-CDs. (B) HRTEM image of D-CDs. Inset shows the lattice spacing. (C) XRD pattern of D-CDs. (D) XPS survey scan. The XPS analysis of (E) C 1s and (F) O 1s of the D-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of D-CDs before and after adding NaBH4. (B) FTIR spectra of D-CDs before and after reaction with NaBH4. The XPS analysis of (C) C 1s, and (D) O 1s of the D-CDs after NaBH4 treatment. Conditions: [D-CDs] = 5 % v/v, [NaBH4] = 50 mmol/L and pH 8.0 (BR buffer).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of D-CDs after adding 0 to 50 μmol/L of HSO3 − with diverse concentrations. (B) Fluorescence photos when adding different concentrations of HSO3 − under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). (C) Color coordinates (x, y) of D-CDs with HSO3 − concentrations from 0 to 50 μmol/L are displayed in the CIE chromaticity diagram. (D) The relationship between the ratio of F 435/F 520 and HSO3 − concentration. The inset exhibits the linear correlation between the F 435/F 520 and the concentration of HSO3 −. (E) Absorption spectra of D-CDs after adding different concentrations (0–50 μM) of HSO3 −; inset: images of the D-CDs solution without and with the addition of HSO3 −. (F) Normalized intensity of D-CDs coexisting with different potentially interfering species. (3 mmol/L: F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, H2O2, 200 μmol/L ClO−, 3 mmol/L Hcy, 2 mmol/L Cys, 250 μmol/L GSH, 250 μmol/L HS−, and 250 μmol/L S2−).

(A) FTIR spectra of D-CDs before and after response with HSO3 −. (B) Fluorescence lifetimes of D-CDs at 520 nm emission without and with HSO3 −. (C) Reversibility of fluorescence assay under diverse pH conditions. The inset exhibits the relative fluorescence pictures of the solutions at different pH conditions. (D) Schematic diagram of the nucleophilic addition reaction between D-CDs and HSO3 −.
Schematic representation of the detection of HSO3 − by smartphone imaging fluorometry of HSO3 − and the linear relationship between G/B and HSO3 − concentration of the portable sensing platform.
Schematic diagram of fabrication of aldehyde-functionalized D-CDs and the principle and application of ratiometric detection of HSO3 −.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of aldehyde-functionalized dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of bisulfite in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisulfite (HSO3 −) is used as a food additive for its antibacterial and antioxidant properties. However, excessive intake of HSO3 − is harmful to humans. Here, for the first time, aldehyde-functionalized dual-emissive carbon dots (D-CDs) are synthesized in one-step for direct ratiometric sensing of HSO3 −. Due to the nucleophilic addition reaction between HSO3 − and aldehyde of D-CDs, the fluorescence transforms from green to deep-blue. The linear range of the probe is 0.1–30 μmol/L with a detection limit of 42 nmol/L. Moreover, D-CDs show good selectivity and a fast reaction time (<5 min) toward HSO3 −. The probe has been applied to trace HSO3 − detection in food samples. The recoveries range from 96.5 % to 107.0 % with relative standard deviations below 6.5 %. In addition, a smartphone sensing platform has been designed, which provides a wider application prospect for the real-time monitoring of HSO3 − in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisulfite (HSO3 −) was first formulated as a food additive in Japan in 1948. Since then, it has been widely used in food products in many countries around the world for its good preservative, antioxidant and bleaching properties (Hayasaka, Black, Hack, & Smith, 2017; Tan et al., 2021). As a good preservative, HSO3 − can be used as an inhibitor of enzymatic reactions during the storage period of fruits such as grapes to prevent the growth of bacterial molds, thus providing a preservation effect (Taylor, Higley, & Bush, 1986). In addition, it is used as a bleaching agent and an antioxidant to keep foods such as dried fruits and sugars from browning or oxidation (Feng et al., 2021). However, if HSO3 − is used in excess, it can seriously damage the nutrients in the food and reduce the nutritional value of the food. Moreover, scholars discovered that ingestion of excess HSO3 − might cause allergic reactions in some individuals, trigger asthma, and even lead to myocardial ischemia, lung cancer, etc., which result in irreversible bodily damage (Jiang et al., 2022). Based on these properties of HSO3 −, the addition of low levels of sulfites to some foods (below the Food Organization/World Health Organization regulation of 0.7 mg kg−1 body weight) is permitted under a strict control (Santos-Figueroa et al., 2013). Considering these factors, a simple, novel, fast, and reliable method for detecting HSO3 − is urgently needed. In the past, numerous methods have been developed to detect HSO3 −, for instance, flow injection analysis (Hassan, Hamza, & Mohamed, 2006), chromatography (Xing et al., 2021), colorimetric determination (X. Song et al., 2021), electrochemical analysis (F. Liu et al., 2017), and fluorescence determination (Yue et al., 2017). Fluorometry is favored because of its fast speed and high sensitivity. At the same time, simple and portable smartphones are also used by researchers as part of analytical methods, combining with colorimetric (A. Shahvar, Saraji, Gordan, & Shamsaei, 2019; Ali Shahvar, Shamsaei, & Saraji, 2020) and fluorescence (Yu, Tan, & Cunningham, 2014) methods for analytical determination. The use of smartphones as a camera or processing unit offers the advantages of miniaturization and ease of operation for quantitative or qualitative analysis in analytical assays, which makes smartphones have wide application prospects in analytical testing.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as popular fluorescent sensors because of their high quantum yield, low toxicity, and good water solubility. However, most of the CDs-based fluorescent sensors are single-responsive toward targets (Bogireddy, Cruz Silva, Valenzuela, & Agarwal, 2020; Dang, Ganganboina, & Doong, 2020). It should be noted that sensing with a single fluorescent signal is inevitably affected by external factors, including the detection environment and background signal, leading to experimental errors (Gao et al., 2021; Jia, Peng, Yang, Wei, Zhang, & Wang, 2021). In view of these limitations, direct use of dual-emissive CDs (D-CDs) as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor provides the following advantages: On the one hand, self-calibration improves detection accuracy by overcoming background signal interference, and on the other hand, the variation of different peak intensities broadens the visualization of the detection process (W. Song et al., 2017). Therefore, in recent years, ratiometric fluorescence sensing techniques for analysis and detection have emerged.

Whereas, because there are no specific optically tunable groups bound to the recognition site, many CDs with common carboxyl and hydroxyl groups can only interact with analytes via simple non-specific surface groups through surface chelation, limiting their accuracy (Chen, Liu, Yu, & Wang, 2019; Y. Wang, Lao, Ding, Zhang, & Liu, 2019). As a result, it is imperative to develop CDs with specific surface functional groups by simple methods. For instance, a study by Xia's group synthesized CDs in one step using phenylboronic acid as the only precursor, introducing the binding site without any surface modification. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched based on the specific covalent bonding between the boronic acid groups on CDs surface and the cis-diol of glucose (Shen & Xia, 2014). Zhang's group chose itaconic acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors to synthesize blue fluorescent CDs with unsaturated bonds (CC), and constructed a sensing method to detect phenylephrine (PHE) using bromine as a bridge. The electrophilic addition of bromine to CC quenches the fluorescence, and when PHE is contained in the system, it can react with bromine by substitution, thus restraining the fluorescence quenching (C. Zhang et al., 2021). It is noteworthy that the one-step preparation of CDs with functional groups avoids the complexity of secondary construction, improves the selectivity of the reaction and provides an understanding of the quenching mechanism of CDs fluorescence. Inspired by these works, we wondered whether CDs synthesized using other small molecules with reactive sites as carbon sources would have the same reactivity in detecting HSO3 −.

Herein, we obtained effective D-CDs via a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, and for the first time, aldehyde-based D-CDs were directly used for the specific and visual assay of HSO3 − (Scheme 1 ). The color of fluorescence turned from green to deep-blue with increasing HSO3 − concentration. Meanwhile, the quenching process and luminescence mechanism were fully studied. The quenching of green fluorescence can be described as the nucleophilic addition reaction between HSO3 − and aldehyde moieties on the surface of D-CDs. Additionally, an interesting pH-mediated reversible fluorescence-switching sensor was developed for the sensitive sensing of HSO3 −. This probe was further used for the determination of HSO3 − in food samples. Moreover, the smartphone can detect HSO3 − instead of the naked eye in the field, providing real-time monitoring and improving data accuracy. Consequently, the sensor has promising application prospects for the detection of HSO3 − in food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification procedures for aldehyde-functionalized D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.2 mmol of 2, 4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde was dispersed ultrasonically in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and the solution pH was brought to 9 with 0.5 mol/L NaOH. The mixture was then moved into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (25 mL) and heated in the oven at 180 °C for 10 h (Shen & Xia, 2014). After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large agglomerates. In the following steps, the solution was moderated to neutral pH with dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) and then diluted 10 times using ultrapure water. After removing impurities by dialysis with deionized water, the D-CDs were stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for ratiometric fluorescence determination of HSO3 −
ce:section.ce:para: 500 μL of BR buffer (0.04 mol/L, pH 8.0) and 50 μL of D-CDs were added to microtube (produced by Eppendorf, 1.5 mL). Various concentrations of HSO3 − were added in this series of microtubes above, and then the mixtures were diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. After shaking well and incubating continuously for 5 min, these solutions were tested. Fluorescence emission spectra were tested in the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of HSO3 − in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: All food samples were purchased at a supermarket in Chongqing. A wolfberry sample (2 g) was weighed, chopped, and then soaked in 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by stirring for 30 min. Hawthorn, dried mango, and grape samples were operated as above. A granulated sugar sample (2 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. All food samples were then filtered through 0.22 μm filters, and all filtrates were collected and analyzed. The HSO3 − content of each sample was determined by taking 20 μL of each sample and adding HSO3 − (0, 2, 5 and 15 μmol/L). Fluorescence spectral data were collected under the same conditions as those described above for spectral measurements.

The Whatman filter papers were drilled into small circles with a 6 mm drill bit. 5 μL of D-CDs solution was dropped on the filter paper to form HSO3 −-responsive paper sensor array. After dried in the ventilation for 10 min, 5 μL of various amounts of HSO3 − (1–50 μmol/L) was dropped onto the small circles, and then air-dried again for 10 min under environmental conditions. Herein, the imaging process was performed in a fixed location in the darkroom, the UV light was placed on a black box with an opening on top, and the imaging location of the smartphone was immobilized. The color of the filter paper was captured using the rear camera of a smartphone camera under the UV light (365 nm). The relevant parameters for smartphone imaging were fixed to obtain the best experimental results and reproducibility. Shutter speed and ISO level were fixed at 60 ms and 3200, respectively. The images were captured under automatic white balance mode. In addition, the captured images were in 8-bit JPEG format with an average size of 0.96 MB (2448 × 3264 pixels). The image analysis was performed on a RGB analysis software (Color Collect, Version 2.5.19) for smartphones. After opening the image in the software, the fluorescent image was converted to the corresponding RGB values, and the G/B value (where G and B are the values of the green and blue visible images, respectively) of the image was considered as the analysis signal.

To the best knowledge of all, the characteristic reaction between –CHO and HSO3 − can be described as a nucleophilic addition. Yin’s group developed coumarin derivatives for the detection of HSO3 −. In this molecule, the aldehyde group was a sensitive reactive site for HSO3 −, which was recognized by the nucleophilic addition reaction (Yue et al., 2017). Meng's group reported a fluorescent probe based on the coumarin backbone, which specifically recognized HSO3 − using the nucleophilic addition reaction between the aldehydes and HSO3 − (Yu et al., 2016). Zhang's group explored a ruthenium(II) complex luminescent probe (Ru-CHO) for the selective recognition of HSO3 − (Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, we were curious whether the carbon dots with aldehyde groups on the surface also have such good sensing properties. Based on this, we attempted 2, 4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde as a sole precursor for the one-step synthesis of carbon dots with aldehyde-rich surface. Encouragingly, the two emission peaks of D-CDs at 435 and 520 nm were observed under a single excitation. And the aldehyde groups on D-CDs served as a recognition site to specifically recognize HSO3 −, thereby changing the fluorescent color from green to deep-blue. Collectively, from molecular-level aromatic aldehydes to nano-scale CDs, this designed probe not only possesses the advantages of tunable excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs, but also retains the original surface aldehyde structure, which can broaden the use of CDs for the application of specific identification assays.

In order to confirm the nanoscale morphology of the D-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were analyzed. In Fig. 1 A, the D-CDs are spherical and evenly dispersed, and size monodispersity was calculated by measuring the size of about 200 particles with an average diameter of approximately 2 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). The high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM), as presented in Fig. 1B and the inset, clearly resolved a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (1 0 0) face of graphite. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows that D-CDs have a distinct peak at approximately 21° in Fig. 1C, which was caused by disordered carbon phase, whereas the peak near 43° indicated 0.21 nm interlayer spacing (which is consistent with HRTEM) (Rao et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019).The UV–vis absorption spectrum of D-CDs (Fig. S1) shows peaks at 274 and 326 nm, which are related to different sp2 domains from the CC and CO π-π* transitions of D-CDs, respectively, and it is attributed to the n-π* transition of the surface CO bond that causes the characteristic absorption peak at 491 nm (Zhang, Tan, & Hu, 2021). The composition of the D-CDs was then determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. S2, the absorption at 3430 cm−1 was derived from OH stretching vibration. From 2935 to 2815 cm−1, stretching vibrations of CH have been observed (Lu, Xu, & Liu, 2019). A typical value for the CO stretching vibration on the aromatic ring was 1080–1160 cm−1, and the absorption at 1500–1407 cm−1 was generated by the conjugation of aromatic rings and CO (Guo et al., 2022). 1602 cm−1 was the location of the CO absorption band, and D-CDs exhibited an absorption peak at 1360 cm−1, indicative of carboxylate groups (COONa) on the surface (Dai, Chen, Ouyang, & Hu, 2022). On the survey scan XPS pattern (Fig. 1D), C 1s (47 %), O 1s (38 %), and Na 1s (14 %) were detected. These bands were located at 284.8, 531, and 1071 eV, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1E) displays three peaks, including CC/CC at 284.8 eV, CO at 286.4 eV, and CO at 288.6 eV. According to Fig. 1F, the peaks at 531.5, 533.4, and 536.0 eV match CO, CO, and COONa in the O 1s spectrum (Liu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the optical properties of D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have inherent optical properties that are usually determined by their own characteristics, so it is essential to investigate the optical properties of D-CDs. A ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed by observing the emission behavior of D-CDs under various excitations at 300–400 nm (Fig. S3), and the optimal excitation wavelength was chosen to be 360 nm for the best visualization. It was also found that there was no excitation-dependent shift in the fluorescence emission peaks under different excitations. It shows bright green fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light (left inset of Fig. S3), and the solution color shows green in visible light (right inset of Fig. S3). Then, the effect of pH change on the fluorescence emission was studied. As pH changed from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S4A), the blue fluorescence grew continually. This is because the UV–visible absorption at 435 nm decreases and the absorption at 491 nm increases with growing pH (Fig. S4B), leading to the absorption spectrum overlaps less with the emission spectrum around the blue emission channel, in the cases, the intramolecular resonance energy transfer capacity is reduced, which results in a rise in the blue fluorescence emission peak. Furthermore, since NaBH4 is a reducing agent that can selectively reduce CO, it was applied to elucidate the fluorescence emission mechanism. The absorption and fluorescence of D-CDs changed significantly after reduction by NaBH4. As observed in Fig. S5, the absorption at 491 nm declines completely, meanwhile, the fluorescence at 520 nm almost disappears and the one at 435 nm is enhanced (Fig. 2 A). It is possible that a decrease in intramolecular resonance energy transfer is responsible for the increase in fluorescence at 435 nm (Liu et al., 2017). The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2B) show that the addition of NaBH4 causes the intensity of CO to become 1/7 of the original, a decrease in the conjugate peaks of the CO and the aromatic ring at 1500–1407 cm−1, as well as a broadening and by a factor of 3 increase in the absorption band of CH2 (L. Wang et al., 2014). In the XPS analysis (Fig. 2C, D), the CO content of D-CDs drops from 26 % to 15 %, and its CO content rises from 7 % to 12 % after the reaction with NaBH4 in C 1s. The same trend is seen in the O 1s (Table S1), indicating that the CO was successfully reduced by NaBH4. This phenomenon was consistent with that of FTIR and confirmed the successful reduction of CO in D-CDs. The photostability of the synthesized D-CDs was measured by recording the fluorescence intensity every 5 min under continuous UV excitation. Good fluorescence stability is observed within 30 min (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral responses of D-CDs toward HSO3 −
ce:section.ce:para:
Observations made in the above study found that D-CDs with active –CHO functional groups on their surface displayed dual-emissive characteristics at 435 as well as 520 nm under the excitation at 360 nm. Therefore, a novel fluorescence detection method was constructed to perform HSO3 − ratiometric sensing. As shown in Fig. S7A and B, after the addition of 50 μmol/L HSO3 −, the UV–vis absorption spectrum displays the disappearance of absorption at 489 nm and in the fluorescence spectrum, the fluorescence at 520 nm decreases, whereas that at 435 nm remains unchanged, so the assay was feasible. As far as we know, the direct ratiometric sensing of HSO3 − employing D-CDs is not yet reported. The experimental conditions were then optimized for obtaining the desired ratiometric fluorescence response results. First, a study was conducted to survey the influence of pH value on HSO3 − detection. The fluorescence intensity is highly pH-dependent in Fig. S8. The fluorescence intensity ratios at 435 and 520 nm (F 435/F 520) grew with the increasing of pH, and Δ(F 435/F 520) peaks at pH 8.0 after the addition of HSO3 −, while F 435/F 520 fluorescence intensity ratios of D-CDs-HSO3 − dropped rapidly when the pH exceeds 8.0, indicating that the binding of D-CDs to HSO3 − does not occur in over-alkaline environments. Therefore, considering a satisfactory burst efficiency of the constructed sensor, pH 8.0 is chosen for the whole experiment. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 435/ F 520) increased with response time after the addition of HSO3 −, and the value stabilized within 5 min (Fig. S9), suggesting a rapid reaction process, which was advantageous for real-time assays. The effect of temperature on D-CDs and D-CDs-HSO3 − was investigated at various temperatures. The result shows that the sensing system has good stability from 20 to 60 °C. However, the fluorescence intensity signal F 435/F 520 of D-CDs-HSO3 − decreased slightly with increasing temperature, which may be due to the volatilization of HSO3 − by decomposition to SO2 (Fig. S10). So the experiment was done at room temperature. Under the above optimal experimental conditions, the dual-emissive fluorescence and colorimetric signals of D-CDs after adding various concentrations of HSO3 − were recorded. As the HSO3 − concentration increased (0–50 μmol/L), the emission peak intensity at 520 nm progressively declined, whereas the emission peak intensity of 435 nm remained invariable (Fig. 3
A). Of particular note, the solution shows a clear green to deep-blue color change with increasing HSO3 − concentration under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3B), which can be identified with the naked eye. And the CIE chromaticity was in accordance with the spectral variation coordinates (Fig. 3C). Additionally, Fig. 3D illustrates the relationship between the ratio of F 435/F 520 and the variation of HSO3 − concentration. The probe shows a linear range of 0.1 to 30 μmol/L with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.9947 (inset of Fig. 3D), and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 42 nmol/L based on 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve). The determination of HSO3 − level can also available in colormetric mode. As revealed in Fig. 3E, D-CDs show a strong absorption signal at 489 nm. The intensity of the absorption peak at 489 nm gradually decreased after adding HSO3 − (0–50 μmol/L) and a clear color reduction from light green to colorless can be observed (Fig. 3E, inset). Moreover, considering the complexity of real samples, the desirable anti-interference property was critical for the developed sensor. Thus, the fluorescence responses of the sensor for some essential potentially interfering analytes were studied in the absence and presence of HSO3 −, including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, S2−, HS−, Cys, GSH, Hcy, H2O2, ClO−, NO3 − and CO3 2−. Firstly, the effect of interfering species on the blank signal was investigated with different concentrations of interfering species. As portrayed in Fig. 3F, the blank signal did not change significantly even when the concentration of these interferents (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, and H2O2) reached 3 mmol/L, whereas 200 μmol/L ClO−, 3 mmol/L Hcy, 2 mmol/L Cys, 250 μmol/L GSH, 250 μmol/L HS−, and 250 μmol/L S2− resulted in a 10 % change in the blank signal, which may be due to the oxidation or reduction of D-CDs by these interferents. In addition, the effect of interferents on the sample signal was investigated. As shown in Fig. S11, for the standard solution containing 30 μmol/L HSO3 −, the data obtained showed no significant change in the sample signal even when the concentration of interferents including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Hcy and Cys reached 3 mmol/L. While the addition of 1.2 mmol/L CO3 2−, 500 μmol/L H2O2, 70 μmol/L ClO−, 2 mmol/L GSH, 250 μmol/L HS−, and 250 μmol/L S2− resulted in a 10 % change in the sample signal. This may be due to the oxidation or reduction of HSO3 − and D-CDs by interfering substances, which inhibits the nucleophilic addition reaction. When the concentration of the above interferents reached 2–100 times of HSO3 −, the signal was basically unchanged or only changed by 10 %, indicating that the sensing method exhibits good analytical performance. Furthermore, the detection performance of our as-prepared sensor is preferable to most of the previously reported fluorescent sensors. As shown in Table S2, the limit of detection of this method is lower than that of other methods. Moreover, the detection of bisulfite using ratiometric fluorescence can overcome the background signal. In addition, the probe is heavy metal-free and environmentally friendly.

Herein, the response mechanism of this ratiometric fluorescent sensor to HSO3 − was systematically studied. As displayed in Fig. 4A, CO stretching vibration peak intensity at 1602 cm−1 falls to 1/6 of its previous value after HSO3 − treatment, and the conjugation peak of the aromatic ring with CO at 1500–1407 cm−1 is also reduced, which means that the double bond and conjugation properties of the CO bond are destroyed by HSO3 −, leading to the reduction of green fluorescence. In the XPS, an increase in CO content can be seen in the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra following adding HSO3 −, which implies that CO is strongly bound to HSO3 − in D-CDs, making CO into CO (Fig. S12). At the same time, the data in Table S3 further confirm the fact that CO is converted to CO (Fu et al., 2020). After adding HSO3 −, the fluorescence lifetimes of the D-CDs decline from 4.12 to 3.66 ns with an 11.2 % change rate, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of the D-CDs occurs dynamically (Fig. 4B), which may be due to the destruction of CO and the formation of addition groups that prohibit n-π* conversion, resulting in the attenuation of the fluorescence lifetime of D-CDs (Fan et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2022). The 1H NMR spectra depict the vanishing of the peak between 4 and 5 ppm after the addition of HSO3 − (Fig. S13), while the peak between 4 and 5 ppm was reported as an aldehyde peak on the CDs surface. This suggests that the nucleophilic addition of HSO3 − to –CHO leads to the disappearance of the aldehyde group. (Ahmed, Abualnaja, Ghareeb, Ibrahim, Abdelsalam, & Emam, 2021; Ostadhossein, Benig, Tripathi, Misra, & Pan, 2018). The 1H NMR results were coincident with those of XPS and FTIR analysis. The above findings suggest that the decline in fluorescence at 520 nm can be described as the addition reaction of CO bond with HSO3 −. Furthermore, a pH-mediated fluorescence reversible experiment capable of detecting HSO3 − was also a firm evidence for the above-mentioned findings (Fig. 4C). Before each fluorescence spectroscopy measurement, NaOH and HCl were alternately added to the solution for pH reversibility measurements. This fluorescent nanosensor shows promising reversibility, that is, the fluorescence of the D-CDs is modulated reversibly for more than four consecutive cycles by pH, and there is no obvious fatigue result. Therefore, it is concluded that the detection mechanism for HSO3 − is the result of the nucleophilic addition reaction between –CHO and HSO3 − on the surface of D-CDs, resulting in a change in fluorescence from green to deep-blue (Fig. 4D).

In food chemistry, HSO3 − is used as an antioxidant and preservative, but excessive use can negatively affect human health. Hence, D-CDs were used to detect HSO3 − in food samples of wolfberry, Hawthorn, dried mango, grape, and granulated sugar to evaluate the practicality. Then a standard addition method was employed to detect HSO3 −, each sample was spiked with 2, 5 and 15 μmol/L HSO3 −, respectively. The recoveries of HSO3 − range from 96.5 to 107.0 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are below 6.5 % (Table S4), which implied that the proposed sensor is capable of determining HSO3 − in food samples and has potential applicability in monitoring food quality.

Smartphone imaging can be employed as a vigorous tool in the orientation of analytical chemistry, greatly facilitating the naked eye detection version. In terms of the obvious change in the fluorescence color of D-CDs, we present an available paper-based sensor for the field measurement of HSO3 − with the assistance of smartphone imaging. Firstly, a series of HSO3 − solutions (1–50 μmol/L) were added dropwise to the prepared indicator paper and photographed with a smartphone camera under day light and a 365 UV lamp. Subsequently, a smartphone software (Color Collect) was used to recognize the color of the acquired image, which was then converted to the corresponding RGB value. Finally, the G/B value (where G and B are the values of the green and blue visible images, respectively) was selected as the ordinate, and the HSO3 − concentration was chosen as the abscissa in order to develop a linear relationship graph. As depicted in Fig. 5 , with the addition of HSO3 − (1–50 µmol/L) into the paper, the color of the paper alters from light yellow to colorless, and the fluorescence color alters from green to blue. And the linear equation for the relationship between G/B and the concentration of HSO3 − can be expressed as follows: G/B = − 0.0066C(HSO3 −) + 1.098, R 2 = 0.9943. And two smartphones of other brands (Honor Play and Xiaomi 10) were used to verify the applicability of the method. All three smartphones showed good linearity in the range of 1–50 μmol/L with coefficients of determination (R 2) of 0.9943, 0.9962, and 0.9900, and the limits of detection of 0.97, 0.91, and 0.91 μmol/L, respectively, indicating that there were no significant differences in the determination of bisulfite among the different phones (Fig. S14). All experiments were performed three times with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.3 %. Moreover, stability tests were carried out. The circular filter papers dripping with D-CDs solution were left at room temperature for 0–3 days and the G/B values of the filter paper remained essentially unchanged with less than 1 % attenuation (Fig. S15). These results reveal that this portable smartphone sensing system has great possibilities of application for in situ HSO3 − semi-quantitative inspection.

Overall, we summarize our contributions as follows: D-CDs with reactive –CHO bonds as functional groups were successfully prepared by one-step synthesis for detecting HSO3 − by ratiometric fluorescence. As expected, one-step synthesis of D-CDs using the aldehyde-containing small molecule as carbon source preserved abundant aldehyde reaction sites on the surface. And the D-CDs were used for the first time for the fluorescence ratiometric detection of HSO3 − through the nucleophilic addition reaction. Meanwhile, the detection has good sensitivity and selectivity, and is successfully applied to the food samples. Furthermore, on the basis of fluorescent color change from green to deep-blue under single excitation, by combining a ratiometric fluorescent probe with a smartphone, a portable smartphone platform was constructed for visual detection of HSO3 −, which offers great potential for real-time visual sensing in the field. In general, we propose a novel idea of synthesizing D-CDs with promising sensing properties, which is expected to tap the potential of more functional CDs for analytical detection in food.

Cui Cui Deng: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zi Yi Xu: Visualization, Investigation. Zhe Sun: Validation, Software. Jia Hao Xie: Data curation, Software. Hong Qun Luo: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Nian Bing Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj- zdxmX0003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134961.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
